intro: What Airstream owners actually add — the upgrades that come up again and again across owner forums, installers, and the Airstream Club. Each item carries a community-consensus signal (how widely owners recommend it, based only on sources we could open and read), is tagged factory or aftermarket, and links the sources behind it. Prices are typical 2025–2026 US street prices for reference, not quotes; verify before buying. This is an independent reference and is not affiliated with Airstream, Inc.

categories.title: Power: lithium, solar & inverters
categories.blurb: The single most-recommended upgrade path. Airstream sells solar and lithium from the factory, sized to each model line (table below) — and going aftermarket unlocks far bigger off-grid builds. Either way, lithium + solar + an inverter is what turns a hookup-only trailer into a boondocking one.

items.name: Lithium (LiFePO4) house batteries
items.consensusNote: Named by multiple owners in the top-mods thread, has its own dedicated multi-owner conversion thread, and led the Outside owner's build.
items.why: 2–3× the usable capacity of lead-acid (100% depth of discharge vs ~50%), far lighter, ~10-year life, and they charge faster. Airstream now sells lithium from the factory as a heated 200Ah (~2.5 kWh) bank — standard on Classic and Pottery Barn, optional on Globetrotter, International, Flying Cloud, Caravel, Bambi and Basecamp — but only bundled with the solar package. The Trade Wind goes furthest with a standard 810Ah heated bank.
items.popular: Airstream's own factory cells are Battle Born — the Trade Wind runs three heated Battle Born units for its 810Ah bank. Aftermarket, Battle Born 100Ah (~$799, 10-yr warranty) is the default and the Epoch Elite V2T (up to 460Ah, built-in Class-T fuse, internal heater) is the installer high-end. Budget drop-ins (Power Queen, LiTime) run ~$230–280 per 100Ah.

sources.label: Airforums — owners' top 3–5 mods (lithium leads the lists)
Airforums — converting a Flying Cloud to lithium + solar
Airstream — Optional Features (factory lithium tiers, by model)
Battle Born — official product/price

items.name: Rooftop solar (factory 100–600W, or bigger aftermarket)
items.consensusNote: Owners run 510–600W roof setups; the rooftop-vs-portable tradeoff is a genuine, unsettled debate in the threads, not a solved question.
items.why: Keeps the battery bank topped up off-grid so you can stay out for days without a generator. Airstream matches factory solar to the model: 600W standard on Trade Wind, 300W on Classic, 300W optional ($2,600) on Globetrotter/International/Flying Cloud, and 100–200W on the smaller Caravel, Bambi and Basecamp — with a 360W tier on Basecamp when you add lithium. See the table above for the full per-model breakdown.
items.popular: The factory 300W package (three 100W panels + controller + interior battery monitor) is the easy box-tick. Owners going bigger fit 600–1000W aftermarket for serious boondocking; some prefer a portable/ground panel to chase the sun in shaded sites, with 'nowhere to store them' the recurring complaint. Many run both.

sources.label: Airforums — owners list 510–600W roof solar among top mods
Airforums — long rooftop-vs-portable solar discussion
Airstream — Optional Features (per-model solar tiers)

items.name: Inverter / inverter-charger (2000–3000W)
items.consensusNote: Loved by the heavier-build crowd; the Outside owner deliberately skipped one in favor of a portable power station, so it's not universal for casual campers.
items.why: Runs household AC outlets — coffee maker, microwave, TV — off the battery bank when you're not plugged in. A pure-sine 2000–3000W unit covers most loads; pair with lithium and solar for a complete off-grid system. The Trade Wind already ships a 3,000W inverter standard with every outlet inverted; on other trailers it's an aftermarket add.
items.popular: Victron MultiPlus / MultiPlus-II 12V 3000VA (US 50A model ~$1,088–1,375) is the near-universal installer choice, usually with a Victron Lynx distributor, Orion XS DC-DC charger and a Cerbo GX touchscreen for monitoring.

sources.label: Airforums — owners cite a hybrid inverter/charger (Victron) as a core add
NAZ Solar Electric — Victron MultiPlus-II 12/3000/120 (UL458) price

items.name: DC-DC charger (alternator charging)
items.consensusNote: Almost always discussed as a required companion to a lithium swap, not a standalone headline mod.
items.why: Lets the tow vehicle's alternator safely charge a lithium bank while you drive, without overloading a modern 'smart' alternator or blowing the 7-pin fuse. Owners call it best practice once you switch to lithium because the factory converter alone charges slowly.
items.popular: Victron Orion XS 1400 is the current favorite in installer builds.

sources.label: Airforums — owners call a DC-DC charger (Orion XS) best practice for lithium
Victron Energy — Orion XS / DC-DC chargers

items.name: Lithium-profile converter / charger
items.consensusNote: Owners stress it constantly as part of any lithium swap — the stock converter won't fully charge LiFePO4.
items.why: The factory WFCO/converter is tuned for lead-acid and stops short of fully charging lithium. Swapping to a lithium-profile converter (or a multi-stage charger) lets shore power and the generator actually top the bank to 100%. A standard companion to any lithium conversion.
items.popular: Progressive Dynamics and WFCO lithium-profile converters are the common drop-in replacements; bigger builds fold this into the Victron inverter/charger instead.

sources.label: Airforums — owners pair lithium with a lithium-profile converter

items.name: Battery monitor / shunt
items.consensusNote: Repeatedly cited as part of any lithium/solar build so you can trust your state-of-charge.
items.why: Counts amps in and out to give a true state-of-charge reading instead of a guesswork voltage gauge — essential with lithium, whose flat voltage curve hides how full the bank really is. Reads on your phone over Bluetooth.
items.popular: Victron SmartShunt 500A (~$80–130) is the default; the Victron BMV-712 adds a dedicated display. Pairs with the rest of a Victron system.

sources.label: Airforums — owners add a shunt/monitor to lithium builds
Victron Energy — SmartShunt (official)

table.title: Airstream's factory solar & lithium program, by model
table.intro: Airstream matches solar wattage and lithium capacity to each model line. Two rules to know: factory lithium is only sold bundled with the solar package, and on Basecamp it requires the 360W solar tier. Everything below is from Airstream's own feature pages.
rows: Standard off-grid build; 3,000W inverter, every outlet inverted
Lithium only with the solar package
table.note: The 10.3/17.2 kWh “Advanced Power System” is a Model-Year-2026 motorhome platform (Atlas, Interstate) — it is not offered on travel trailers, so it's deliberately left off this list.

sources.label: Airstream — Optional Features Across the Travel Trailer Lineup (solar & lithium tiers)
Airstream Support — factory option list & 300W package price
Airstream — All-New Trade Wind (810Ah / 600W / 3,000W standard)
Airstream — World Traveler features (300W + 2.5 kWh heated lithium wording)

categories.title: A/C, airflow & climate
categories.blurb: Making the air conditioner run off batteries or a small generator, moving air without it, and keeping the trailer comfortable in heat and cold.

items.name: Soft start for the A/C
items.consensusNote: Appears by name in owner top-mod lists; installers report thousands of installs.
items.why: A rooftop A/C draws 60–100+ amps the instant the compressor kicks on, which trips breakers and stalls inverters. A soft start cuts that surge by ~70% (down to ~25–30A), so you can run the A/C off lithium + inverter, or start it on a small 2,000W generator or a 15/20A household plug. Owners call this near-mandatory if you want A/C off-grid.
items.popular: Micro-Air EasyStart 364 and SoftStartRV are the two enthusiast standards. Needed on each A/C unit if you have two.

sources.label: Airforums — owners run A/C off a generator with a soft start
Micro-Air EasyStart — manufacturer

items.name: MaxxFan / MaxxAir powered roof vent
items.consensusNote: Named by 3+ separate owners in the top-mods thread as a Fantastic/Magic-Fan replacement.
items.why: A powerful 10-speed roof fan moves enough air to cut how often you need the A/C, and the built-in rain shield lets you leave it open in a downpour — so you can vent cooking heat and humidity even while parked in the rain. Owners swap the stock Fantastic/Magic Fan for it.
items.popular: MaxxFan Deluxe 7500K (remote, thermostat, built-in rain cover) is the one owners name; a MaxxShade or insulated pillow cover pairs with it for cold nights.

sources.label: Airforums — owners replace the Fantastic fan with a MaxxFan
Maxxair (Airxcel) — MaxxFan, official

items.name: Second A/C with heat pump
items.consensusNote: A factory-order spec rather than a community-driven mod — it didn't surface in the owner mod threads, so it's here as an option to know about, not a popular add.
items.why: Adds cooling capacity for hot climates or larger floorplans and boosts the electrical system to 50 amps. Factory option on several International and Globetrotter floorplans (not all — e.g. excluded on the 30RB). The heat-pump function also extends shoulder-season heating without burning propane.
items.popular: Factory-only; most owners who camp in the South add it when ordering. Can't be retrofitted easily, so decide at order time.

sources.label: Airstream Support — factory option list & price

categories.title: Towing & safety
categories.blurb: The gear that keeps the trailer tracking straight and protects against the most common road and campground hazards. Forums and the Airstream Club treat the first items as non-negotiable.

items.name: Brake controller
items.consensusNote: The Airstream Club lists it first on its carry list and notes it's legally required for towing trailer brakes.
items.why: Operates the trailer's electric brakes in proportion to the tow vehicle, so the rig stops as one unit instead of the trailer shoving the truck. Legally required to use trailer brakes in most states, and the foundation of safe towing. Many newer trucks have one built in; otherwise it's a quick add.
items.popular: Tekonsha Prodigy P3 is the long-standing proportional standard; built-in (integrated) controllers on newer trucks are fine too.

sources.label: Airstream Club — 36 Things Every Airstreamer Should Carry (#1, brake controller)

items.name: Weight-distribution + anti-sway hitch
items.consensusNote: Treated as a foundational safety given rather than an optional upgrade.
items.why: Distributes tongue weight across all axles and fights trailer sway — the biggest single towing-safety upgrade. Airstream's long wheelbase tows well, but a proper WD/anti-sway hitch transforms stability, especially in crosswinds and truck passes.
items.popular: Equal-i-zer 4-point (integrated sway, ~$500–700) is the value standard. ProPride 3P (Jim Hensley design, $2,995–3,695) is the no-sway premium pick enthusiasts swear by for larger trailers. Size the model to your loaded hitch weight.

sources.label: ProPride 3P — official store & price
Equal-i-zer 4-point — official product page

items.name: Tire pressure monitoring system (TPMS)
items.consensusNote: In the Airstream Club's 'Highly Recommended' tier, called out by the TST brand name.
items.why: Trailer tires fail far from the driver's seat; a TPMS alarms on pressure or temperature before a blowout that can shred a fender and bodywork. Universally recommended by the Airstream Club and owners.
items.popular: TST 507 (flow-through or cap sensors, color monitor; 6-sensor kit ~$446) is the enthusiast standard; a signal repeater is recommended for travel-trailer length.

sources.label: Airstream Club — 36 Things Every Airstreamer Should Carry (TPMS, TST)
TST 507 6-sensor — retail price

items.name: Surge protector / EMS
items.consensusNote: Airstream Club 'Highly Recommended'; owners mention hardwired Progressive 50A EMS by name.
items.why: Campground pedestals deliver miswired, low, or surging power that can fry the converter, A/C and electronics. A surge protector blocks spikes; a full Electrical Management System also disconnects on low/high voltage and wiring faults. Owners consider this cheap insurance.
items.popular: Progressive Industries EMS (portable or hardwired EMS-HW50C) and Hughes Autoformer are the recommended names; match 30A or 50A to your trailer.

sources.label: Airstream Club — 36 Things Every Airstreamer Should Carry (EMS, Progressive)
Airforums — owners run a hardwired Progressive 50A EMS

items.name: Leveling blocks & wheel chocks
items.consensusNote: Blocks/chocks are in the Airstream Club's Essential tier; among the most-named basics by owners.
items.why: Campsites are rarely level, and the absorption fridge works best level. Curved drive-on levelers plus solid chocks are the fast, reliable setup the Airstream Club and owners recommend.
items.popular: Andersen Levelers are the most-recommended drive-on levelers; pair with X-chocks (dual-axle trailers) or standard chocks (single-axle).

sources.label: Airstream Club — 36 Things Every Airstreamer Should Carry (leveling, chocks)

items.name: LevelMate Pro (Bluetooth leveling sensor)
items.consensusNote: A community darling — one owner called it 'probably my most favorite improvement'; named repeatedly alongside levelers.
items.why: A small box you mount inside the trailer that shows on your phone exactly how far off level you are, front-to-back and side-to-side — so you place blocks right the first time instead of guess-and-check. Pairs with leveling blocks above; owners rave about how much setup time it saves.
items.popular: LogicBlue LevelMate PRO+ is the named product, mounted near the axle and read over Bluetooth.

sources.label: Airforums — owners call the LevelMate their favorite improvement
LogicBlue — LevelMate PRO, official

categories.title: Water & plumbing
categories.blurb: Cleaner water in, better pressure, less water used off-grid, and protecting the plumbing.

items.name: Inline / upgraded water filter
items.consensusNote: In the Airstream Club's Highly Recommended tier and named by owners (Blu Tech, Seagull, Acuva).
items.why: Removes sediment, chlorine taste and impurities from campground water. Owners who care about taste step up from cheap blue inline filters to a higher-flow multi-stage system, often with a separate drinking filter inside.
items.popular: Basic Camco inline for most; the Airstream Club and owners call out Blu Technology / Clearsource multi-stage filters plus a Seagull/Acuva drinking filter for serious filtration.

sources.label: Airstream Club — 36 Things Every Airstreamer Should Carry (water filter)
Airforums — owners name Blu Tech / Seagull / Acuva filters

items.name: Oxygenics shower head + full cutoff valve
items.consensusNote: Owners specifically praise the Oxygenics head and a full-cutoff handle; the older 'recirc valve' framing was more creator-driven.
items.why: A high-pressure, low-flow shower head with a true cutoff valve in the handle lets you do a 'navy shower' — full pressure, then off, without losing the temperature mix — which stretches fresh water and slows grey-tank fill off-grid. One of the most-loved cheap mods.
items.popular: The Oxygenics shower head ('great pressure, uses hardly any water') plus a head with a separate complete cutoff is the combo owners name.

sources.label: Airforums — owners' favorite: Oxygenics head + full cutoff

items.name: Water pressure regulator
items.consensusNote: Universally owned but assumed baseline gear — rarely celebrated as a 'mod', just expected.
items.why: Campground water pressure can spike high enough to burst RV plumbing lines. An adjustable regulator with a gauge protects the system — a cheap, universally recommended first buy that's more 'baseline gear' than passion upgrade.
items.popular: Adjustable brass regulators (e.g. Renator, Valterra) with a gauge are the common pick.

sources.label: Pagosa Supply — water hose & filter essentials

items.name: Composting toilet (OGO / Nature's Head)
items.consensusNote: Polarizing — the Outside owner's single favorite upgrade, but a love-it-or-leave-it choice. Boondockers love it; many owners keep the standard black tank.
items.why: Replaces the flush toilet and black tank with a waterless composting unit that separates liquids from solids. Saves fresh water, eliminates dumping the black tank, and frees up clearance and cargo — a passion upgrade for serious dry-campers. Trade-offs: you empty the liquids often and it's a real plumbing change, so it's not for everyone.
items.popular: OGO (compact, electric mixer) and Nature's Head (the long-standing standard) are the two names owners install; some do a full 'black-tank delete'.

sources.label: Outside — owner's favorite upgrade: composting toilet / black-tank delete
OGO — composting toilet, official

categories.title: Interior, lighting & comfort
categories.blurb: The small, popular quality-of-life mods owners do in the first season — most are cheap DIY.

items.name: Mattress upgrade or topper
items.consensusNote: The factory mattress is a near-universal complaint; owners swap or top it early.
items.why: The stock mattress is widely considered too firm and too thin; a replacement or a good topper is one of the most common first-season comfort fixes, especially for full-timers. Custom RV sizes mean a standard mattress rarely fits, so owners buy RV-cut.
items.popular: Airstream Supply Co. sells trailer-cut mattresses; owners also use a latex/memory-foam topper or a Beddy's zip-around set sized for the bed.

sources.label: Airforums — owners swap the factory mattress
Airstream Supply Co. — trailer-cut mattresses & bedding

items.name: LED lighting conversion
items.consensusNote: Cheap, universal, repeatedly done — the Outside owner converted all lights to LED; older rigs especially.
items.why: Swapping incandescent/halogen bulbs for warm-white LEDs cuts lighting power draw by ~90% (a big deal off-grid) and reduces heat. A cheap, satisfying first mod — mostly relevant to older trailers, since current Airstreams already ship LED.
items.popular: Direct-fit 921/1141 warm-white LED replacements; owners pick warm white (~3000K) to keep the cozy look.

sources.label: Outside — owner converted all lighting to LED
Airforums — warm-white LED bulb swaps in owner mod lists

items.name: Window film / tint
items.consensusNote: Owners list tinted front windows among top mods to 'stop the heat'.
items.why: Airstream's wraparound windows let in a lot of heat and sun; tint cuts cabin heat and glare and adds daytime privacy. Recurring item on 'upgrades I'd do again' lists.
items.popular: Professional ceramic tint is the common route; some owners also swap the door's screen-guard panel for clear glass for better visibility.

sources.label: Airforums — owners list window tint + clear door glass

items.name: Glass skylight upgrade
items.consensusNote: The 'Maxim' glass skylight is named by multiple owners in the top-mods thread.
items.why: Replaces the stock plastic dome with a clear glass skylight for a better view and a more premium feel. Popular cosmetic + light upgrade.
items.popular: Maxim Skylights is the brand owners name.

sources.label: Airforums — owners install Maxim glass skylights

items.name: Anti-theft / hitch & coupler locks
items.consensusNote: A steady security basic — owners mention coupler/chrome locks; not a hot debate, but widely owned.
items.why: Locks the coupler and hitch so a thief can't simply tow the trailer away, hitched or unhitched. Recommended security basic.
items.popular: Airstream's Anti-Theft Lock Kit or a heavy-duty coupler lock (e.g. Trimax) on the hitch.

sources.label: Airstream Supply Co. — deadbolt hitch & coupler locks

items.name: 170° soft-close cabinet hinges
items.consensusNote: A common DIY 'fix-it' rather than a headline passion mod — popular in owner fix lists, lower signal in the top-mods threads.
items.why: Stock cabinet doors only open partway; swapping to 170° hinges gives full access to closets and lockers. Cheap, high-satisfaction DIY fix.
items.popular: 170° soft-close hinges (Amazon/ROK) — a top community 'fixes' recommendation.

sources.label: Airforums — owner cabinet/closet fixes

items.name: Stainless exterior trim & rock/window guards
items.consensusNote: Low community heat — one weak owner mention; mostly a cosmetic/protective preference.
items.why: Swap plastic exterior parts for stainless for durability and shine; padded window and stone guards protect the shell and windows from road debris. Airstream sells both as customization kits.
items.popular: Airstream's stainless upgrade bundle (sold in pieces or as a complete set) and stone/window guard kits — no drilling.

sources.label: Airstream Supply Co. — stainless steel upgrade bundles
Airstream — Customization Guide
